metadata.title: Assessing the Merits of Decentralization: A Framework for Identifying the Causal Mechanisms Influencing Policy Outcomes
keywords: State of the State Address Palabras Clave: Agendas políticas
Discurso State of the State 政策议程

abstract: This study tests the effect of gubernatorial policy agendas on state budgetary choices. Governors are commonly perceived as state policy leaders, but lack direct legislative authority and depend on the law makers to pass bills and present them for signature. The theory posits that governors nonetheless possess several formal and informal tools that are used to overcome this institutional disadvantage and successfully pursue their policy goals. Gubernatorial policy priorities in this study are extracted from public speeches using unsupervised machine learning. Their influence on state spending choices in different policy areas is tested through panel data analysis of all states from 2007 through 2015. Empirical findings reveal that the effect of executive agendas is more pronounced in the area of transportation infrastructure and public safety-potentially due to lower levels of conflict and concentrated benefits, which eases deal making with law makers. Governors are also more persistent with policy proposals that are more important for reelection.

sections.header_raw: Prioridades de política gubernativa y resultados fiscales estatales
sections.header_norm: prioridades de pol tica gubernativa y resultados fiscales estatales
paragraphs: Este estudio prueba el efecto de las agendas de políticas gubernamentales en las elecciones presupuestarias estatales. Los gobernadores son comúnmente percibidos como líderes de políticas estatales, pero carecen de autoridad legislativa directa y dependen de los legisladores para aprobar proyectos de ley y presentarlos para su firma. La teoría postula que los gobernadores, no obstante, poseen varias herramientas formales e informales que pueden utilizarse para superar esta desventaja institucional y perseguir con éxito sus objetivos políticos. Las prioridades de la política gubernamental en este estudio se extraen de los discursos públicos mediante el aprendizaje automático no supervisado. Su influencia en las elecciones de gasto estatal en diferentes áreas de políticas se prueba a través del análisis de datos de panel de todos los estados desde 2007 hasta 2015. Los resultados empíricos revelan que el efecto de las agendas ejecutivas es más pronunciado en el área de la infraestructura de transporte y la seguridad pública potencialmente debido a niveles más bajos de conflicto y beneficios concentrados, lo que facilita el trato con los legisladores. Los gobernadores también pueden ser más persistentes con las propuestas de políticas que son más importantes para la reelección.

Next I present the empirical findings before engaging in a discussion of the empirical findings and drawing pertinent conclusions.

sections.paper_id: Ivonchyk - 2020 - Gubernatorial Policy Priorities and State Fiscal Outcomes
paragraphs: The power of state chief executives has been analyzed in previous research. Comparative case studies date back as far as the 1930s (Lipson 1939). More recently, scholars have employed different techniques to test gubernatorial influence in state policy making. Early surveys of state department heads indicated that they perceived legislatures, not governors, as having more impact on the programs and objectives of state government departments (Abney and Lauth 1983). Subsequent surveys of executive and legislative budget officers first found that the appropriation process was dominated by governors (Abney and Lauth 1987), but later suggested a perceived decline of gubernatorial dominance with relative parity between the executive and legislative powers in the appropriation process (Abney and Lauth 1998). These findings led to the conclusion that executive dominance, present since the early part of the twentieth century, had been declining by the end of the century. Other scholars also found a more affirmative role of state legislatures vis-à-vis state chief executives in budgeting matters during that period (Thompson 1987). Dometrius and Wright (2010), however, used survey data from the 1970s, 1980s, and 1990s and found no decline in gubernatorial influence on state agency budgets. Instead, their results suggest that, on average, governors slightly increased their influence vis-à-vis legislatures from the 1980s through the 1990s. ryu and others (2008) relied on survey data from 1998 and provided similar findings pointing toward gubernatorial dominance in the budgeting process. Another survey of legislative and executive budget analysts in Western states in 2000-01 also found governors, not legislatures, to be the main beneficiaries of any influence shifts (Goodman 2007). There is some evidence that power between the two branches is not a zero-sum game, where one institution is gaining influence at the expense of the other (Dilger, Krause, and Moffett 1995). This divergence of findings with respect to what branch dominates the policy-making process may be explained by different operationalizations of key variables and by a reliance on perceptive measures.

A more detailed analysis reveals that gubernatorial effectiveness depends on both institutional and environmental factors (Dilger, Krause, and Moffett 1995). Governors with more institutional powers in the budgetary process tend to increase the type of spending that confers statewide benefits and aids their political ambitions (Barrilleaux and Berkman 2016). Executive budgetary powers may also be related to state budget punctuations irrespective of legislative constraints and party control (Breunig and Koski 2009). There is some evidence that state budgets grow at a higher rate when executive unilateral control over fiscal policy making increases, which implies that more balanced budgetary powers between the two branches may be necessary to restrain excessive spending (Krause and Melusky 2012).

Kousser and Phillips (2012) tested gubernatorial success with budgetary proposals and policy bills more directly. Policy agenda items were manually extracted from the State of the State Addresses. Their findings suggest that governors are more successful with budgetary-rather than policy-proposals due to their more powerful formal stance in the budgeting negotiations. The main shortcoming of this study, however, is that "due to the onerous nature of tracking gubernatorial proposals" data were limited to a nonrandom sample of 28 states over two legislative sessions-2001and 2006(Kousser and Phillips 2012, 76). Thus, whereas the research by Kousser and Phillips is a substantial improvement over the previous efforts to understand gubernatorial influence, the empirical findings may not be generalizable to the states excluded from the analysis.

Studies exploring presidential influence in the legislative process provide other notable insights. To achieve their policy goals, presidents strategically choose to publicize the issues that Congress would not otherwise support (Canes-Wrone 2001), particularly their own initiatives (Barrett 2005). This points to the ability of chief executives to promote their policy agenda by using the "bully pulpit" unavailable to other, less prominent, public officials. This tool can influence Supreme Court decisions (rogol, Montgomery, and Kingsland 2018) and public opinion (Cohen 1995;Cohen and Hamman 2003), although this effect may be diminishing (Edwards 2006;young and Perkins 2005). Presidential success in the legislative arena also varies by policy area and requires careful prioritization (Peake 2001). There is some evidence, for example, that presidents are less active in pursuing their policy goals in the area of health care and social welfare potentially due to a higher level of conflict and required compromise, which increases the cost of legislative action (Cummins 2008). Little is known, however, concerning the extent to which the success of state chief executives varies by policy area.

To summarize, there have been multiple attempts to understand executive influence in state policy making, but much of this line of research has been either cross-sectional or relied on a short time period or used crude measures of policy agenda. This study seeks to advance current research by using computational text analysis to provide a more nuanced measure of gubernatorial policy priorities over nine years for all 50 states. unlike cross-sectional studies, these results should provide useful information in terms of longitudinal analysis and should be generalizable to all states. Additionally, this research uses disaggregated expenditures to test gubernatorial influence in different policy areas. The next section lays out the theory of interbranch bargaining and provides the hypotheses for empirical testing.

sections.paper_id: Ivonchyk - 2020 - Gubernatorial Policy Priorities and State Fiscal Outcomes
paragraphs: American governors are ideologically flexible (Willoughby 2014), but "whatever the policy preferences of the chief executives, they generally want more power to impose those preferences on the budget" (rubin 2016, 11). This endeavor becomes increasingly challenging in a contemporary public budgeting process characterized by intense partisanship and gridlocks to the point where government shutdowns have become more common and last longer (rubin 2016). Given their lack of direct legislative authority and the current environment, how might governors pursue their policy goals? under what conditions could they be more successful?

This research adopts a theoretical framework of rational choice developed in previous studies of gubernatorial powers (Gray, Hanson, and Kousser 2017;Kousser andPhillips 2009, 2012;Sigelman and Dometrius 1988). The theory describes how the state budgeting process should unfold. The core assumption of this framework is that state chief executives and legislators are both rational actors seeking to achieve their diverse policy goals, but neither has complete control. The process proceeds through bargaining and negotiations. Whether they want to pass a budget or a policy bill, governors depend on the legislators who, in turn, may be interested in side payments and favors that governors can deliver in return for cooperation.

The office of state chief executive provides an array of formal and informal tools that can be utilized to a governor's advantage. All governorships are wellpaid, full-time jobs, whereas many state law makers are part time and poorly paid, and some might meet only for a short period of time. Many legislatures are not as well staffed as the offices of state chief executives. Governors have more experience, knowledge, and time to develop an effective bargaining strategy. They are the most prominent individual state officials with ample opportunities to address wide audiences. Experienced governors remind newcomers that this "bully pulpit" is one of the most powerful weapons in their arsenal (National Governors Association 2015). "When a governor speaks, whether to the press, the legislature, on the road or through responses to constituent inquiries, focus is immediate" (National Governors Association 2015, 77). The inaugural address, State of the State Address, and budget message can be "incredibly useful and powerful communication tools" (National Governors Association 2015, 77).

State chief executives can use this visibility to cultivate support for reelection and fundraising efforts of individual law makers. Seasoned governors note that this is done in several ways, from inviting legislators to parties at the executive mansion to joint appearances in electoral districts (National Governors Association 2010). Some also gave legislators departmental and agency bills to sponsor so that they could claim credit (rosenthal 2012). Governors can make favorable appointments and hire legislators' political allies, especially at the beginning of an administration and after reelection. uncooperative law makers and their campaigns, however, can be publicly attacked and criticized.

Governors have the flexibility of choosing to sign or veto the bills important to law makers. By these means a state chief executive can make legislators' "lives happier and their careers brighter" (rosenthal 2012, 124), or the opposite.

This theoretical framework describes the main strategies state chief executives can utilize to overcome the institutional disadvantage in law making vis-à-vis legislators. With these tools at their disposal, a gubernatorial policy agenda can be carried out through policy-focused legislative or budgetary bills. Admittedly, a policy bill incorporating executive priorities would have a more lasting effect on state polices. It is expected, however, that state chief executives have less leverage over policy bills and might be more successful with their policy agenda in the budgeting game for several important reasons. The next subsection outlines this aspect in more detail.

Governors have the flexibility of choosing to sign or veto the bills important to law makers. By these means a state chief executive can make legislators' "lives happier and their careers brighter" (rosenthal 2012, 124), or the opposite.

This theoretical framework describes the main strategies state chief executives can utilize to overcome the institutional disadvantage in law making vis-à-vis legislators. With these tools at their disposal, a gubernatorial policy agenda can be carried out through policy-focused legislative or budgetary bills. Admittedly, a policy bill incorporating executive priorities would have a more lasting effect on state polices. It is expected, however, that state chief executives have less leverage over policy bills and might be more successful with their policy agenda in the budgeting game for several important reasons. The next subsection outlines this aspect in more detail.
sections.text_with_footnotes:
American governors are ideologically flexible (Willoughby 2014), but "whatever the policy preferences of the chief executives, they generally want more power to impose those preferences on the budget" (rubin 2016, 11). This endeavor becomes increasingly challenging in a contemporary public budgeting process characterized by intense partisanship and gridlocks to the point where government shutdowns have become more common and last longer (rubin 2016). Given their lack of direct legislative authority and the current environment, how might governors pursue their policy goals? under what conditions could they be more successful?

This research adopts a theoretical framework of rational choice developed in previous studies of gubernatorial powers (Gray, Hanson, and Kousser 2017;Kousser andPhillips 2009, 2012;Sigelman and Dometrius 1988). The theory describes how the state budgeting process should unfold. The core assumption of this framework is that state chief executives and legislators are both rational actors seeking to achieve their diverse policy goals, but neither has complete control. The process proceeds through bargaining and negotiations. Whether they want to pass a budget or a policy bill, governors depend on the legislators who, in turn, may be interested in side payments and favors that governors can deliver in return for cooperation.

The office of state chief executive provides an array of formal and informal tools that can be utilized to a governor's advantage. All governorships are wellpaid, full-time jobs, whereas many state law makers are part time and poorly paid, and some might meet only for a short period of time. Many legislatures are not as well staffed as the offices of state chief executives. Governors have more experience, knowledge, and time to develop an effective bargaining strategy. They are the most prominent individual state officials with ample opportunities to address wide audiences. Experienced governors remind newcomers that this "bully pulpit" is one of the most powerful weapons in their arsenal (National Governors Association 2015). "When a governor speaks, whether to the press, the legislature, on the road or through responses to constituent inquiries, focus is immediate" (National Governors Association 2015, 77). The inaugural address, State of the State Address, and budget message can be "incredibly useful and powerful communication tools" (National Governors Association 2015, 77).

State chief executives can use this visibility to cultivate support for reelection and fundraising efforts of individual law makers. Seasoned governors note that this is done in several ways, from inviting legislators to parties at the executive mansion to joint appearances in electoral districts (National Governors Association 2010). sections.paper_id: Ivonchyk - 2020 - Gubernatorial Policy Priorities and State Fiscal Outcomes
paragraphs: Fiscal, political, and legal realities require both branches to come to the negotiation table (Kousser and Phillips 2009). Only eleven states have mechanisms for passing a temporary budget if the main budget is late (rubin 2009), but even these measures cannot become a permanent replacement for a new budget and temporary budget solutions also cut deeply into the approval ratings of both branches (Kousser and Phillips 2009). In most states, a budget delayed past the start of the fiscal year triggers an automatic shutdown (Kousser and Phillips 2012). Thus, in budgetary negotiations both branches bear the cost of delays and have a considerable incentive to compromise. In addition to political costs, there are substantial financial costs because late budgets significantly increase the cost of state borrowing (Andersen, Lassen, and Nielsen 2014). Late budgets raise concerns in the investment community concerning stable repayments of outstanding debt and are viewed by the municipal bond market as a sign of bad fiscal governance. When repayments become uncertain, investors require a corresponding compensation for higher risks. Consequently, both sides are incentivized to compromise in the budgeting realm.

The consequence of failing to come to an agreement and pass a governor's policy bill, however, means that state policies simply retain the status quo. In a typical case there are no noticeable interruptions in government operations. Governors are left with a take-it-or-leave-it option in this realm or, at least, a weaker negotiating position (Kousser and Phillips 2012). In addition, negotiations and bargaining for a policy bill may continue over several years. In such situations not being able to succeed in the first legislative session does not necessarily mean failure for a state administration. 1Another advantage of governors in the budgeting process stems from the fact that most states use some form of executive budgeting, where governors have constitutional responsibility for formulating budgetary proposals Ivonchyk / GuBErNATOrIAL POLICy PrIOrITIES AND FISCAL OuTCOMES | 497 (rosenthal 2012). In this model, the governor's office provides budget instructions to the agencies, collects their proposals, trims them as necessary, and forwards the whole budget as a recommendation to the legislature for consideration (rubin 2016). By virtue of the fact that governors create the first draft, their policy priorities form the basis for the allocation of state resources and set the initial framework for budget deliberations. Depending on fiscal constraints, governors get to make the initial decision as to what policy initiatives and programs receive more, less, or no funding.

Beyond setting the initial parameters on who gets what, executive budgeting enables governors to include pork projects for legislators in their proposals (or supplemental appropriations) to cultivate legislative support for the whole package (rubin 2016). Taken together, these factors improve the gubernatorial bargaining stance in the budgeting process as compared to negotiations over policy bills. Indeed, the National Governors Association (2015) advises governors to use budgets strategically as an important vehicle for achieving key gubernatorial objectives. The relative balance of budgeting powers between the two branches varies, but most states lie between the extremes of executive or legislative dominance with an average governor having more authority than legislators (rubin 2016).

Thus, this theory of interbranch bargaining suggests that generally governors possess several formal and informal tools to overcome this institutional disadvantage in the law-making process. Fiscal, political, and legal realities also imply that governors might be more successful with their agenda in the budgeting process. Given this framework, the main expectation is that gubernatorial agendas will have a significant effect on state budgetary outcomes.

sections.paper_id: Ivonchyk - 2020 - Gubernatorial Policy Priorities and State Fiscal Outcomes
paragraphs: Governors have flexibility in terms of their policy agenda so their party labels may not be particularly meaningful in terms of policy priorities (Kousser and Phillips 2012; rubin 2016). They have a wide range of options when it comes to their policy agendas (rosenthal 2012). The origins of policy items and the exact moment of agenda formation are impossible to trace. Most governors state that the promises and commitments made during electoral campaigns are the primary source of their first year's policy goals, and that their agenda, in general, is a function of personal experiences, values, and political philosophies (rosenthal 2012). research at the national level also suggests that chief executives respond to public opinion and media coverage (Cohen 1997;Eshbaugh-Soha and Peake 2005) and in some policy areas this relationship can be reciprocal (Hill 1998).

Whatever the source, "it is popularly believed that agenda completion occurs with the State of the State Address" (rosenthal 2012, 113). This is the highest profile event of the year for the chief executives who get a chance to speak to both chambers of their state's legislature. The event takes place at the outset of the legislative sessions in all states. State officials expect governors to demonstrate their policy leadership by articulating the issues that need attention and by setting the tone for the upcoming legislative session. The process of drafting the speech starts weeks in advance and focuses on major items due to time limitations on the length of the speech (Kousser and Phillips 2012). Many governors believe their success is measured by how many of the State of the State items get passed (rosenthal 2012). As such, the speech represents a serious commitment and they need to finalize their priority list by the time of the speech. Around that time, governors are expected to complete and present their budgets to the legislature. Therefore, the establishment of clear priorities as a prerequisite to budgeting and delivering the speech is paramount (National Governors Association 2015).

Consequently, for the purposes of this work, it is assumed that gubernatorial agenda formulation precedes submission of the executive budget and the State of the State Address. Policy priorities are then built into the recommended budget and divulged to the public in the State of the State Addresses. The executive agenda is an antecedent of both the speech and the budget. This also suggests that the State of the State Address should be a reasonably good gauge of the executive agenda in any given year. Indeed, studies show the topics most frequently mentioned in the addresses (Willoughby 2008(Willoughby , 2009(Willoughby , 2016(Willoughby , 2017) ) match almost one to one to the actual priorities highlighted by the governors in their responses to a survey (rosenthal 2012).

The main aim of this study was to test whether these priorities from the State of the State Addresses have any effect on the budgetary choices made by the legislatures. Submission of the recommended budget is only the first step. It has no effect on the actual distribution of resources unless approved by the law makers. This is where the real test of executive dexterity with formal and informal sticks and carrots begins. If the main hypothesis holds, we should see a significant correlation between gubernatorial policy priorities and adopted budgetary choices. This would indicate an effective use of formal and informal tools to pursue their agenda.

Gubernatorial agendas influence the overall size of state budgets (see e.g., Barrilleaux and Berkman 2016;Kousser andPhillips 2009, 2012), but the effect may vary by policy area, which is impossible to detect with aggregated data. Presidents, for example, are less successful in health care and social welfare policy because these areas are commonly characterized by a higher degree of visibility, conflict, and compromise (Cummins 2008). State chief executives may be similarly less successful in these policy areas due their inability or unwillingness to pursue politically costly policy proposals. Additionally, voters may be more vigilant in holding governors accountable for particular issues, such as public safety (Cummins 2009)-which can be a serious incentive for governors to be more tenacious in these areas. Consequently, the influence of gubernatorial agendas can vary by policy and this study sought to test this possibility at the state level with disaggregated data.

sections.paper_id: Ivonchyk - 2020 - Gubernatorial Policy Priorities and State Fiscal Outcomes
sections.header_raw: Ivonchyk / GuBErNATOrIAL POLICy PrIOrITIES AND FISCAL OuTCOMES | 499
sections.header_norm: ivonchyk gubernatorial policy priorities and fiscal outcomes 499
paragraphs: There can be any number of items on the agenda. However, throughout the last decade most governors have been primarily concerned with the following items: education, health care, public safety, natural resources, and transportation infrastructure (Willoughby 2008(Willoughby , 2009(Willoughby , 2016(Willoughby , 2017)). This list of policy areas corresponds closely to the main functional areas of state governments (u.S. Census 2018). Hence, these five policy areas are selected for this study. This choice ensures that the study is focused on the issues that vary in terms of importance, but nonetheless are applicable to all governors and state governments throughout the nation.

Since the State of the State Address is limited in time (Kousser and Phillips 2012), governors will likely devote more attention to the items that are more important. It is reasonable to assume that governors want to be remembered for the changes they achieve, and most reforms aiming to make a tangible change in the society will require additional resources. Thus, on average, we may expect an increase in the amount of resources devoted to the policy areas that are more important to the governor. However, governors may be interested in a reduction in the amount of resources devoted to a policy area where government involvement is undesirable per their political ideology. State chief executives have used their powers, for instance, to prevent increases (Barrilleaux and rainey 2014) or to cut health-care expenditures (Kousser 2002). In other instances they reduced the level of investment into higher education (Tandberg and Ness 2011). These examples illustrate that more gubernatorial attention to a policy area may be negatively associated with level of spending in that area. Thus, positive as well as negative associations between policy importance and the level of expenditures are plausible. ultimately, the direction of this relationship is an empirical question. If the theoretical framework provides a realistic description of events, we can expect individual policy priorities to be significantly related to the level of state spending by function. More specifically, this proposition leads to the following hypotheses:
Hypothesis 1: Gubernatorial policy priorities in education are significantly related to state expenditures on education.

Hypothesis 2: Gubernatorial policy priorities in health care are significantly related to state expenditures on health care. Hypothesis 3: Gubernatorial policy priorities in transportation infrastructure are significantly related to state expenditures on transportation infrastructure. Hypothesis 4: Gubernatorial policy priorities in public safety are significantly related to state expenditures on public safety.
Hypothesis 5: Gubernatorial policy priorities in natural resources are significantly related to state expenditures on natural resources.

A priori, an expectation here is that the level of spending for some of the categories will be determined by law, be it statutory provisions or judicial precedents, to a larger extent than by the annual budgetary process. For example, the judiciary has effectively required more funding for several state prisons ruling that overcrowding was unconstitutional. The Abbot cases in New Jersey have affected state education expenditures for at least two decades now. Similar cases have had an impact on education spending in Kentucky, Washington, and other states. The level of state spending in these examples was defined by court decisions leaving less space for bargaining and policy priorities of government officials. Much of health-care spending is devoted to Medicaid, where eligibility and benefits are stipulated by law and fluctuate with socioeconomic conditions, not policy preferences. But no two states are the same, and the main question here is whether an average American governor can exert significant influence on their state's budgetary choices-and if so, in what areas. The next section describes the data, variables, and estimation methodology used to explore this question.

If the theoretical framework provides a realistic description of events, we can expect individual policy priorities to be significantly related to the level of state spending by function. More specifically, this proposition leads to the following hypotheses:

Hypothesis 1: Gubernatorial policy priorities in education are significantly related to state expenditures on education.

Hypothesis 2: Gubernatorial policy priorities in health care are significantly related to state expenditures on health care. Hypothesis 3: Gubernatorial policy priorities in transportation infrastructure are significantly related to state expenditures on transportation infrastructure. Hypothesis 4: Gubernatorial policy priorities in public safety are significantly related to state expenditures on public safety.

Hypothesis 5: Gubernatorial policy priorities in natural resources are significantly related to state expenditures on natural resources.

A priori, an expectation here is that the level of spending for some of the categories will be determined by law, be it statutory provisions or judicial precedents, to a larger extent than by the annual budgetary process. For example, the judiciary has effectively required more funding for several state prisons ruling that overcrowding was unconstitutional. The Abbot cases in New Jersey have affected state education expenditures for at least two decades now. Similar cases have had an impact on education spending in Kentucky, Washington, and other states. The level of state spending in these examples was defined by court decisions leaving less space for bargaining and policy priorities of government officials. Much of health-care spending is devoted to Medicaid, where eligibility and benefits are stipulated by law and fluctuate with socioeconomic conditions, not policy preferences. But no two states are the same, and the main question here is whether an average American governor can exert significant influence on their state's budgetary choices-and if so, in what areas. The next section describes the data, variables, and estimation methodology used to explore this question.

sections.paper_id: Ivonchyk - 2020 - Gubernatorial Policy Priorities and State Fiscal Outcomes
paragraphs: Gubernatorial policy priorities represent the main predictor of interest in this study. Policy goals are often inferred from party affiliations, but gubernatorial agenda does not always toe party lines and executive policy goals may be hard to predict without knowledge of the individuals (rubin 2016). Party affiliations are also time invariant and may not capture changes in a gubernatorial agenda over time. Instead, policy priorities for this study were extracted from the State of the State Addresses, which are high profile events taking place in all states. Given the time limits on the address, governors usually choose to focus on the main policy initiatives for the upcoming year (Herzik 1991; Light 1999). These speeches generally contain a wide range of policy statements and allow a much more nuanced measurement of gubernatorial policy priorities in each year than, for example, party labels. The address is intended to influence public opinion as Ivonchyk / GuBErNATOrIAL POLICy PrIOrITIES AND FISCAL OuTCOMES | 503 well as legislative process (rosenthal 1990;Van Assendelft 1997). Law makers, staffers, and former governors universally agree that the addresses contain the most important gubernatorial proposals (Kousser and Phillips 2012). For these reasons it is a superior gauge of executive policy goals (Ferguson 2003) and a good predictor of subsequent gubernatorial actions (Jackson and Kingdon 1992;Segal and Cover 1989). The content of gubernatorial speeches is commonly used to measure governors' policy agendas (Crew 1992;DiLeo 2001;Ferguson 2003;Herzik 1991;Kousser and Phillips 2012;Morehouse 1998).

Other gubernatorial messages are generally shorter and much narrower in scope. The annual or biennial executive budget message, for instance, may also shed some light on executive policy preferences, but its length varies from barely a page, as in California and North Carolina, to multiple pages. Apparently, this statement has a different value to state executives, in some cases only nominal, and serves different goals. Most of the time the budget message focuses on specific issues pertaining to state fiscal performance rather than the overarching policy goals of the administration. It usually provides a summary of the recommended budget, describes revenue and expenditure trends, lays out the main assumptions built into the proposed budget and highlights major areas of concern for state fiscal health. unlike the widely publicized State of the State Addresses, budget messages rarely receive as much public attention and are not intended as major public policy statements. In that respect, the State of the State Address is a superior source of executive policy preferences. Thus, as it has been aptly summed up, "lacking detailed interview or survey data from governors, their major public addresses provide the best insight available into their preferences, values, and ideology" (Coffey 2005, 90).

Earlier studies commonly analyzed the content of text documents by hand with human coders quantitating text into data. Documents were coded based on the number of sentences devoted to a topic (Heidbreder 2012; Heidbreder and Scheurer 2013) or the topic structure was simply assumed based on the reading of the document (Ferguson 2003;Ferguson and Barth 2003). This is a timeconsuming approach that is also prone to errors. This study makes use of the advent of computational text analysis based on unsupervised machine-learning algorithms, which is a more efficient and systematic method of text analysis. Gubernatorial policy priorities for this study were extracted from transcripts of the State of the State Addresses using the Latent Dirichlet Allocation algorithm (LDA) developed by Blei, Ng, and Jordan (2003). The intuition behind LDA is that each document in a collection-often referred to as a corpus-may contain multiple topics. In this case, LDA helps to extract policy themes governors discuss in the State of the State Addresses-the probabilistic co-occurrence of words to discuss the topics is inferred from the corpus. LDA represents each cluster as a collection of words ordered by their probability of appearing in this cluster. Each cluster contains all words from the corpus vocabulary, but the probability of appearing for each word differs within and between clusters. All words' probabilities in each cluster add up to one, so more formally a cluster is defined as a probability distribution over a fixed vocabulary.

The most probable words in each cluster, usually the top five or ten, are used to annotate the cluster with thematic meaning. For example, if the top five words in an LDA cluster are "teacher," "school," "graduation," "tests," "dropout," it becomes clear that this cluster is related to the topic of education and we can designate this cluster as an "education" topic. A thematically unrelated word "airplane," for example, may also be in this cluster, but it will have a much lower probability of appearing in the cluster and will be mentioned toward the bottom of the words list. Since these clusters of words provide meaningful information concerning the thematic content of documents, they are referred to as topics (Blei 2012; Hollibaugh 2019). Each document in the corpus may exhibit any combination of such topics.

The corpus in this study includes 425 State of the State Addresses, or all speeches delivered throughout the nine years of the study in all states. 6 LDA topic modeling does not require prior knowledge of the topics. The only parameter that needs to be defined for proper modeling is the number of distinct topics that we expect to find in the corpus. This number was set to 15 based on the existing research. Namely, Willoughby (2008Willoughby ( , 2009Willoughby ( , 2016) ) manually analyzed the content of the addresses for the same time period and discovered that 15 distinct policy topics were mentioned in the speeches, with two-thirds of governors mentioning about five topics per speech.

Topic distributions over vocabulary from LDA analysis are presented in Table 2 with the 15 most frequent words ordered by their propensity of appearing in each topic. This output documents several distinct policy areas that are discussed in the State of the State Addresses from 2007 to 2015. Topic 13, for example, is clearly concerning education and student performance since some of the most probable words are school, student, education, teacher, graduate, learn, and success. Other topics are annotated in a similar manner using the most common terms (Table 2).

The LDA also estimates the share of each document devoted to each topic (i.e., the distribution of documents over topics). On average, governors devoted about 9 percent of their speeches to the topic of education, but that share varies throughout the sample from about a half of a percent to 36 percent (Table 1). Out of the 15 topics identified by the LDA, I select those that are of primary concern in this study, namely education, health care, transportation infrastructure, public safety, and natural resources. Each of the variables represents the percent of each speech devoted to the corresponding policy area 6 There are 425 speeches because the address is delivered biennially-usually in odd years-in Arkansas, Montana, Nevada, North Carolina, North Dakota, and Texas. These states have biennial legislative sessions. Data from the speech delivered the year before were used for the 25 state-years where the speech was not delivered. Alternative model specification with these 25 states-years excluded from the analysis provided similar results suggesting the decision to use lagged data for these 25 observations does not affect the main findings. and has a theoretical range from 0 to 100 percent of the speech. The higher the share, the more prominent an issue is on the gubernatorial agenda. These are the main predictors of interest. Descriptive statistics for gubernatorial policy priority measures are presented in Table 1.

To validate the LDA results, the topics identified in the computation text analysis can be compared to the list of topics identified by human coders manually analyzing the State of the State Addresses delivered over a comparable period of time (Willoughby 2016). Table 3 lists the topics discovered by manual reading alongside those discovered by LDA.

Those at the top are the topics most frequently mentioned by governors according to Willoughby ( 2016). It appears that LDA discovered most of the topics found by human coders. As we can see, LDA does a good job discovering the more frequent topics, whereas the less frequent ones are not picked up well. Another way to check the validity of the LDA output is to compare the topic distribution to some common-sense expectations. For instance, it is reasonable to expect that more attention will be given to the issue of natural resources in the states that heavily depend on this type of revenue. About 92 percent of all governors dedicated less than 10 percent of their addresses to natural resources. The top 8 percent in terms of attention to natural resources include Alaska,

sections.text:
Gubernatorial policy priorities represent the main predictor of interest in this study. Policy goals are often inferred from party affiliations, but gubernatorial agenda does not always toe party lines and executive policy goals may be hard to predict without knowledge of the individuals (rubin 2016). Party affiliations are also time invariant and may not capture changes in a gubernatorial agenda over time. Instead, policy priorities for this study were extracted from the State of the State Addresses, which are high profile events taking place in all states. Given the time limits on the address, governors usually choose to focus on the main policy initiatives for the upcoming year (Herzik 1991; Light 1999). These speeches generally contain a wide range of policy statements and allow a much more nuanced measurement of gubernatorial policy priorities in each year than, for example, party labels. The address is intended to influence public opinion as Ivonchyk / GuBErNATOrIAL POLICy PrIOrITIES AND FISCAL OuTCOMES | 503 well as legislative process (rosenthal 1990;Van Assendelft 1997). Law makers, staffers, and former governors universally agree that the addresses contain the most important gubernatorial proposals (Kousser and Phillips 2012). For these reasons it is a superior gauge of executive policy goals (Ferguson 2003) and a good predictor of subsequent gubernatorial actions (Jackson and Kingdon 1992;Segal and Cover 1989). The content of gubernatorial speeches is commonly used to measure governors' policy agendas (Crew 1992;DiLeo 2001;Ferguson 2003;Herzik 1991;Kousser and Phillips 2012;Morehouse 1998).

Other gubernatorial messages are generally shorter and much narrower in scope. The annual or biennial executive budget message, for instance, may also shed some light on executive policy preferences, but its length varies from barely a page, as in California and North Carolina, to multiple pages. Apparently, this statement has a different value to state executives, in some cases only nominal, and serves different goals. Most of the time the budget message focuses on specific issues pertaining to state fiscal performance rather than the overarching policy goals of the administration. It usually provides a summary of the recommended budget, describes revenue and expenditure trends, lays out the main assumptions built into the proposed budget and highlights major areas of concern for state fiscal health. unlike the widely publicized State of the State Addresses, budget messages rarely receive as much public attention and are not intended as major public policy statements. In that respect, the State of the State Address is a superior source of executive policy preferences. Thus, as it has been aptly summed up, "lacking detailed interview or survey data from governors, their major public addresses provide the best insight available into their preferences, values, and ideology" (Coffey 2005, 90).

Earlier studies commonly analyzed the content of text documents by hand with human coders quantitating text into data. Documents were coded based on the number of sentences devoted to a topic (Heidbreder 2012; Heidbreder and Scheurer 2013) or the topic structure was simply assumed based on the reading of the document (Ferguson 2003;Ferguson and Barth 2003). This is a timeconsuming approach that is also prone to errors. This study makes use of the advent of computational text analysis based on unsupervised machine-learning algorithms, which is a more efficient and systematic method of text analysis. Gubernatorial policy priorities for this study were extracted from transcripts of the State of the State Addresses using the Latent Dirichlet Allocation algorithm (LDA) developed by Blei, Ng, and Jordan (2003). The intuition behind LDA is that each document in a collection-often referred to as a corpus-may contain multiple topics. In this case, LDA helps to extract policy themes governors discuss in the State of the State Addresses-the probabilistic co-occurrence of words to discuss the topics is inferred from the corpus. LDA represents each cluster as a collection of words ordered by their probability of appearing in this cluster. Each cluster contains all words from the corpus vocabulary, but the probability of appearing for each word differs within and between clusters. All words' probabilities in each cluster add up to one, so more formally a cluster is defined as a probability distribution over a fixed vocabulary.

The most probable words in each cluster, usually the top five or ten, are used to annotate the cluster with thematic meaning. For example, if the top five words in an LDA cluster are "teacher," "school," "graduation," "tests," "dropout," it becomes clear that this cluster is related to the topic of education and we can designate this cluster as an "education" topic. A thematically unrelated word "airplane," for example, may also be in this cluster, but it will have a much lower probability of appearing in the cluster and will be mentioned toward the bottom of the words list. Since these clusters of words provide meaningful information concerning the thematic content of documents, they are referred to as topics (Blei 2012; Hollibaugh 2019). Each document in the corpus may exhibit any combination of such topics.

The corpus in this study includes 425 State of the State Addresses, or all speeches delivered throughout the nine years of the study in all states. 6 LDA topic modeling does not require prior knowledge of the topics. The only parameter that needs to be defined for proper modeling is the number of distinct topics that we expect to find in the corpus. This number was set to 15 based on the existing research. Namely, Willoughby (2008Willoughby ( , 2009Willoughby ( , 2016) ) manually analyzed the content of the addresses for the same time period and discovered that 15 distinct policy topics were mentioned in the speeches, with two-thirds of governors mentioning about five topics per speech.

Topic distributions over vocabulary from LDA analysis are presented in Table 2 with the 15 most frequent words ordered by their propensity of appearing in each topic. This output documents several distinct policy areas that are discussed in the State of the State Addresses from 2007 to 2015. Topic 13, for example, is clearly concerning education and student performance since some of the most probable words are school, student, education, teacher, graduate, learn, and success. Other topics are annotated in a similar manner using the most common terms (Table 2).

The LDA also estimates the share of each document devoted to each topic (i.e., the distribution of documents over topics). On average, governors devoted about 9 percent of their speeches to the topic of education, but that share varies throughout the sample from about a half of a percent to 36 percent (Table 1). Out of the 15 topics identified by the LDA, I select those that are of primary concern in this study, namely education, health care, transportation infrastructure, public safety, and natural resources. Each of the variables represents the percent of each speech devoted to the corresponding policy area 6 There are 425 speeches because the address is delivered biennially-usually in odd years-in Arkansas, Montana, Nevada, North Carolina, North Dakota, and Texas. These states have biennial legislative sessions. Data from the speech delivered the year before were used for the 25 state-years where the speech was not delivered. Alternative model specification with these 25 states-years excluded from the analysis provided similar results suggesting the decision to use lagged data for these 25 observations does not affect the main findings. and has a theoretical range from 0 to 100 percent of the speech. The higher the share, the more prominent an issue is on the gubernatorial agenda. These are the main predictors of interest. Descriptive statistics for gubernatorial policy priority measures are presented in Table 1.

To validate the LDA results, the topics identified in the computation text analysis can be compared to the list of topics identified by human coders manually analyzing the State of the State Addresses delivered over a comparable period of time (Willoughby 2016). Table 3 lists the topics discovered by manual reading alongside those discovered by LDA.

Those at the top are the topics most frequently mentioned by governors according to Willoughby ( 2016). It appears that LDA discovered most of the topics found by human coders. As we can see, LDA does a good job discovering the more frequent topics, whereas the less frequent ones are not picked up well. Another way to check the validity of the LDA output is to compare the topic distribution to some common-sense expectations. For instance, it is reasonable to expect that more attention will be given to the issue of natural resources in the states that heavily depend on this type of revenue. About 92 percent of all governors dedicated less than 10 percent of their addresses to natural resources. The top 8 percent in terms of attention to natural resources include Alaska,

sections.paper_id: Ivonchyk - 2020 - Gubernatorial Policy Priorities and State Fiscal Outcomes
paragraphs: According to theory (Gray, Hanson, and Kousser 2017;Kousser andPhillips 2009, 2012;Sigelman and Dometrius 1988), the ability to deliver favors and credible threats will depend on gubernatorial political capital, which is a function of their popularity, veto powers, and time in office. Lower popularity, holding all else equal, will make joint appearances with governors less attractive. Public criticism is less likely to have an intended effect when governors have low approval ratings. Consequently, less popular chief executives may have fewer favors to offer and will be less influential in the budgeting game. The popularity of governors is measured as a percent of votes received during elections. It varies from 36 to 78 percent, with a mean of 56 percent. Some are relatively unpopular with only a third of voters supporting them whereas others start off with almost 80 percent of support. The disadvantage of this measure is that it remains constant throughout the term, but it is the only proxy for popularity available for all states for the period of the study.

Gubernatorial veto powers can make a governor a more, or less, imposing opponent (Dearden and Husted 1993). The veto powers and other institutional factors, however, are purged by the nature of the fixed effects within estimation because they do not vary over time. Previous studies also found that "executive success wanes over time." This supports the traditional "bank-account" theory of chief executive clout (Ferguson 2003, 172). Legislators may foresee long relationships and be more considerate of gubernatorial priorities during their first term as governors will have plenty of time to pay back for (un)cooperation. Hence, governors may be more successful when it is their first time in office and less successful when serving their last consecutive term. Two dummy variables are introduced to account for time in office. The first is coded as a 1 if a governor is in the first year of office or as a 0 otherwise. The other one is coded as a 1 if a governor cannot be reelected for another term due to reaching the maximum number of consecutive terms in this state or as a 0 otherwise (i.e., legacy term, or "lame duck"). Those in between serve as the comparison category.

The models include controls for a governor's party affiliation (republican is the omitted comparison category) and gender (male is the omitted category). Partisan control of the legislature may be related to gubernatorial success and is measured by which party controls both houses (republican control is the omitted category), or whether the control is split between the two parties. 7Another dummy variable is coded as a 1 when the governor is of the same party as the one that dominates the legislature.

The next set of controls accounts for sociodemographic factors known to influence state fiscal policies (population, population change, percent white, percent population over 65), all provided by the u.S. Census Bureau. State economic conditions can affect the availability of resource and thus ease or complicate the bargaining process. These are controlled for with Gross State Product (GSP) per capita, 8 GSP change, unemployment rate and its change relative to the last year.

Budgetary decisions are made in light of a state's fiscal health. To account for that, the models control for the estimated end balance of the budget in millions of dollars. 9 This estimate gives decision makers an idea on how the current fiscal year is going to end and may influence their preferences for higher or lower spending. Governors may have a harder time pushing their agenda through the legislature when states are facing deficits and running a low-end balance. A count variable representing state credit ratings from S&P (AAA = 12, AA+ = 11, and so on) was also included as an additional control for the overall state fiscal health. Citizen ideological leaning may be related to a state's distribution of resources. State liberalness in this study is proxied by the percent of votes for a Democratic candidate in the most recent presidential elections. This measure, like gubernatorial popularity, remains constant over four years until the next election but is the best available proxy measure for the period of study.

Another control variable is the diversity of the gubernatorial agenda. It could be important because gubernatorial success may depend on focus. A diverse agenda may dilute public and legislative attention and "may overtax the governor's limited political capital" (National Governors Association 2015, 57). Focusing on a limited range of issues, perhaps from three to five, may improve the chances of success, whereas a diverse agenda may be detrimental. Agenda diversity in this study is measured using a Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI), which is equal to the sum of squares of all shares of the speech. For example, if only two issues were discussed in a speech and each took exactly half the speech, the HHI would be .5 (i.e., .5 2 + .5 2 ). If four issues were discussed and each took a quarter of the speech, the HHI would be .25 (i.e., .25 2 + .25 2 + .25 2 + .25 2 ). If two issues were discussed and one took 90 percent of the speech and the other only 10 percent, the HHI would be .82 (i.e., .1 2 + .9 2 ). Higher values of the index represent a more concentrated agenda, and lower values mean a less focused agenda.

The preceding list of control variables represents the common factors that might affect spending in all categories. Each of the functional areas may be affected by additional factors. Education spending, for example, may vary with 8 The other two variables-state poverty level and personal income-were highly collinear with GSP and were dropped. 9 The estimated end balance represents the estimated difference between expenditures and revenues at the end of the current fiscal year. The end balance includes the rainy-day fund balance where applicable. Due to economic-or other-fluctuations, the actual end balance may deviate from the adopted budget. These data were extracted from the Fiscal Survey of the States of the National Association of State Budget Officers (2019).

Ivonchyk / GuBErNATOrIAL POLICy PrIOrITIES AND FISCAL OuTCOMES | 511 the level of school enrollment. This variable, however, was highly collinear (.996) with the population variable and therefore omitted. The number of indigent and retirees may drive state health-care expenditures. These factors should be accounted for with the common variables, such as the percent of the population over 65 and the unemployment rate. The length of public roads managed by the states is included in the model of highway spending. Public safety spending may be correlated with crime rate in the state. The intention was to control for the number of violent and property crimes per 100,000 population in the public safety models. However, these variables tend to be collinear (.6). The results are the same with either variable, but the omnibus fit statistics were better with the property crime rate. Hence, property crimes per 100,000 is used in the public safety model (logged to alleviate positive skew). Natural resources spending may be correlated with the rate of state reliance on severance taxes for revenue. Hence, severance taxes as percent of total tax revenue is included in the natural resources model.

Budgetary decisions are made in light of a state's fiscal health. To account for that, the models control for the estimated end balance of the budget in millions of dollars. 9 This estimate gives decision makers an idea on how the current fiscal year is going to end and may influence their preferences for higher or lower spending. Governors may have a harder time pushing their agenda through the legislature when states are facing deficits and running a low-end balance. A count variable representing state credit ratings from S&P (AAA = 12, AA+ = 11, and so on) was also included as an additional control for the overall state fiscal health. Citizen ideological leaning may be related to a state's distribution of resources. State liberalness in this study is proxied by the percent of votes for a Democratic candidate in the most recent presidential elections. This measure, like gubernatorial popularity, remains constant over four years until the next election but is the best available proxy measure for the period of study.

Another control variable is the diversity of the gubernatorial agenda. It could be important because gubernatorial success may depend on focus. A diverse agenda may dilute public and legislative attention and "may overtax the governor's limited political capital" (National Governors Association 2015, 57). Focusing on a limited range of issues, perhaps from three to five, may improve the chances of success, whereas a diverse agenda may be detrimental. If four issues were discussed and each took a quarter of the speech, the HHI would be .25 (i.e., .25 2 + .25 2 + .25 2 + .25 2 ). If two issues were discussed and one took 90 percent of the speech and the other only 10 percent, the HHI would be .82 (i.e., .1 2 + .9 2 ). Higher values of the index represent a more concentrated agenda, and lower values mean a less focused agenda.

sections.paper_id: Ivonchyk - 2020 - Gubernatorial Policy Priorities and State Fiscal Outcomes
paragraphs: The empirical findings are reported in Tables 4 and 5. The results from the fixed effects within estimation suggest that the main variables of interestgubernatorial policy priorities-are significantly associated with two out of five spending categories. According to the findings, controlling for institutional, political, and socioeconomic factors, budgetary choices in the area of education, health care, and natural resources are not affected by gubernatorial agendas. Highway spending and public safety, however, are significantly associated with gubernatorial policy priorities. The importance of transportation infrastructure and public safety is positively related to state spending on these functions.

More specifically, with each additional percent of the State of the State Address devoted to transportation infrastructure, total state highway spending tends to increase by .5 percent. For an average highway budget of $1.7 billion, this translates into an increase of $8.5 million. An average public safety budget of $830 million tends to increase by .2 percent, or by $1.7 million with each additional percent of the speech devoted to the topic of public safety.foot_6 Given that the average speech is about 4,400 words, 1 percent of the speech is about 50 words, which on average takes about 20 seconds to pronounce. Hence, 10 percent of an average speech will take about three minutes. This implies that if a governor spends about three minutes of the address on the topic, an average increase in total highway spending may be about $85 million and in public safety about $17 million. These findings provide empirical support for Hypotheses 3 and 4. Table 5. sections.paper_id: Ivonchyk - 2020 - Gubernatorial Policy Priorities and State Fiscal Outcomes
paragraphs: The empirical findings indicate that governors are more successful with their policy priorities in the areas of transportation infrastructure and public safety. I observe significant increases in state spending on these functions when these policy topics become more important to state chief executives. The results support previous studies suggesting a significant influence of gubernatorial policy agendas on state budgets. This study, however, provides a more nuanced analysis showing that executive influence varies by policy area. This would not be captured with the total budget used as the dependent variable.

It is important to consider why gubernatorial influence is discovered in some areas but not in others. One possibility is that it is easier in some policy areas to strike mutually acceptable deals with legislators due to a lower level of conflict (Lowi 1964). We saw above that presidential policy proposals are less successful in more contentious areas, such as health care and social welfare (Cummins 2008). The same may be the case at the state level, where debates over health care and education have been ideologically charged. The question often is not only how much to spend, but what is the most appropriate solution. Government-funded health insurance is commonly contrasted with private insurance and public schools with more independent charter schools. Finding common ground here may be much more challenging. The result may be more cooperation between the two branches in the transportation infrastructure and public safety area, where goals and means are clearer and mutually agreeable.

Another explanation is related to the fact that governors and legislators have different electoral incentives. Governors have a statewide constituency and may be interested in funding policies with statewide benefits, whereas legislators are more concerned with policies providing district-specific benefits (Crain 1999;Lewis, Schneider, and Jacoby 2015). Transportation infrastructure and public safety policies tend to have geographically concentrated benefits (Barrilleaux and Berkman 2016). The incentive to cooperate in these areas may be higher for law makers because the outcomes of increased spending will be geographically concentrated and improve their chances to win over their constituency. There is less incentive for them to vote for initiatives with dispersed, statewide effects. Indeed, when given more power, legislatures tend to allocate more spending to the policies benefiting identifiable groups within the states (Lewis, Schneider, and Jacoby 2015). Preferential treatment of some districts for education funding would also be harder to achieve as courts can rule such cases to be in violation of the equal protection clause. Similarly, once eligibility requirements are established for state-funded health-care benefits, they become available to all eligible regardless of their legislative district.

A third possibility is that governors are more persistent with policy proposals in the areas known to affect gubernatorial elections. For instance, voters routinely hold incumbents running for reelection accountable for economic conditions (Niemi, Stanley, and Vogel 1995;Orth 2001;Partin 1995). Others Ivonchyk / GuBErNATOrIAL POLICy PrIOrITIES AND FISCAL OuTCOMES | 515

show that the public safety issue is also a prominent factor in gubernatorial elections (Cummins 2009). The observed variation of gubernatorial influence can thus reflect their strategic decisions to invest more of their finite political capital in the policy proposals known to have greater implications for reelection prospects. At the very least, governors can claim credit for securing additional resources for these policies and show tangible evidence of their efforts.

The budgeting process has also been changing. Scholars estimate that discretionary spending subject to annual or biennial reconsideration ranges between 5 percent and 15 percent of the total budget (rosenthal 2012), with the rest going to mandatory expenditures stipulated by law. As more state resources are absorbed by mandatory spending, there may be fewer opportunities for individual policy makers, including state chief executives, to pursue their policy agenda through the budgetary process. The theory posits that progress is achieved through bargaining and negotiation, but modern budgeting is characterized by increased partisanship, brinkmanship, hostage taking, and gridlock to the point where government shutdowns have become much more common (Fletcher and Weiner 2011;rubin 2016). In such an environment, it may be even harder for state chief executives to provide policy leadership than previously depicted.

As for natural resources expenditures, this dependent variable comprises spending on conservation, promotion, and development of natural resources. Hence, it is impossible to distinguish conservation from development, which would be very helpful. Combined as they are, these expenditures may or may not provide focused benefits. This may explain why on average there is no significant association with gubernatorial priorities.

Scholars estimate that discretionary spending subject to annual or biennial reconsideration ranges between 5 percent and 15 percent of the total budget (rosenthal 2012), with the rest going to mandatory expenditures stipulated by law. As more state resources are absorbed by mandatory spending, there may be fewer opportunities for individual policy makers, including state chief executives, to pursue their policy agenda through the budgetary process. The theory posits that progress is achieved through bargaining and negotiation, but modern budgeting is characterized by increased partisanship, brinkmanship, hostage taking, and gridlock to the point where government shutdowns have become much more common (Fletcher and Weiner 2011;rubin 2016). In such an environment, it may be even harder for state chief executives to provide policy leadership than previously depicted.

As for natural resources expenditures, this dependent variable comprises spending on conservation, promotion, and development of natural resources. Hence, it is impossible to distinguish conservation from development, which would be very helpful. Combined as they are, these expenditures may or may not provide focused benefits. This may explain why on average there is no significant association with gubernatorial priorities.

The empirical findings indicate that governors are more successful with their policy priorities in the areas of transportation infrastructure and public safety. I observe significant increases in state spending on these functions when these policy topics become more important to state chief executives. The results support previous studies suggesting a significant influence of gubernatorial policy agendas on state budgets. This study, however, provides a more nuanced analysis showing that executive influence varies by policy area. This would not be captured with the total budget used as the dependent variable.

It is important to consider why gubernatorial influence is discovered in some areas but not in others. One possibility is that it is easier in some policy areas to strike mutually acceptable deals with legislators due to a lower level of conflict (Lowi 1964). We saw above that presidential policy proposals are less successful in more contentious areas, such as health care and social welfare (Cummins 2008). The same may be the case at the state level, where debates over health care and education have been ideologically charged. The question often is not only how much to spend, but what is the most appropriate solution. Government-funded health insurance is commonly contrasted with private insurance and public schools with more independent charter schools. Finding common ground here may be much more challenging. The result may be more cooperation between the two branches in the transportation infrastructure and public safety area, where goals and means are clearer and mutually agreeable.

Another explanation is related to the fact that governors and legislators have different electoral incentives. Governors have a statewide constituency and may be interested in funding policies with statewide benefits, whereas legislators are more concerned with policies providing district-specific benefits (Crain 1999;Lewis, Schneider, and Jacoby 2015). Transportation infrastructure and public safety policies tend to have geographically concentrated benefits (Barrilleaux and Berkman 2016). The incentive to cooperate in these areas may be higher for law makers because the outcomes of increased spending will be geographically concentrated and improve their chances to win over their constituency. There is less incentive for them to vote for initiatives with dispersed, statewide effects. Indeed, when given more power, legislatures tend to allocate more spending to the policies benefiting identifiable groups within the states (Lewis, Schneider, and Jacoby 2015). Preferential treatment of some districts for education funding would also be harder to achieve as courts can rule such cases to be in violation of the equal protection clause. Similarly, once eligibility requirements are established for state-funded health-care benefits, they become available to all eligible regardless of their legislative district.

A third possibility is that governors are more persistent with policy proposals in the areas known to affect gubernatorial elections. For instance, voters routinely hold incumbents running for reelection accountable for economic conditions (Niemi, Stanley, and Vogel 1995;Orth 2001;Partin 1995). sections.paper_id: Ivonchyk - 2020 - Gubernatorial Policy Priorities and State Fiscal Outcomes
paragraphs: The main conclusion of this study is that governors indeed are influential players in state policy making as depicted in the previous literature. However, the evidence presented here suggests that their influence varies by policy area and is more pronounced in transportation infrastructure and public safety. There are several possible explanations for this variation. It may be easier to strike deals with the legislature in these areas with lower levels of conflict or because the benefits from these policies are geographically concentrated. Governors could also be more persistent with their policy proposals in these areas because of greater implications for reelection (Cummins 2009).

These findings provide a more nuanced view of gubernatorial influence in the legislative process with some implications. The theory of interbranch bargaining describing the state policy-making process should consider dissimilarities between policy subsystems and potential implications for gubernatorial success. It has been noted that differences across policy areas within one system of government may be greater than differences across governments (Breunig, Koski, and Mortensen 2010;Freeman 1985;Lowi 1972;Smith 2002). As a result, a theory of the policy-making process can be accurate in one policy area but less useful in another. The current theoretical framework focuses on overall gubernatorial success and does not explicitly address this possibility, whereas the findings presented here suggest this to be the case.

From a practical point of view, the lack of universal influence is a positive finding suggesting a balance of power between the two branches. Because more unilateral power to governors leads to a higher rate of budget growth (Krause and Melusky 2012), having a reasonable balance might prevent excessive spending. From a governor's perspective, it is reasonable to be mindful at the agenda setting stage of the different levels of difficulty they may encounter in different policy areas. Prior studies show that governors may survive difficult times and maintain legislative success by reducing the ambition of their policy agendas (Kousser and Phillips 2012). This research complements this literature by highlighting the areas where gubernatorial success is more likely.

There are some opportunities for future research. This study makes a step forward by using data disaggregated by function, but future studies should test the theory with more granular data. A more detailed picture would require data by agency, department, or individual programs. Whereas this research finds no relationship with education, health care, and natural resources-there may be changes within the function with the totals not affected. It would also be beneficial to see where the funds governors secure are spent. Future researchers should also note the utility of computational text analysis techniques in analyzing and quantitating the content of text documents. This study demonstrates how an LDA algorithm can be applied to public statements to extract meaningful policy topics. The automated methods could be particularly useful for individual researchers as they may allow u.S. to overcome the high costs commonly associated with manual content analysis of large collections of documents.
